perturbation.original_text: For purposes of this Agreement, the terms below shall have the following meanings: (a) “Confidential Information” shall mean: (i) with respect to NEW RIVER, information as defined generally in section l(a)(ii) following, as well as information, in any format, whether written, oral, visual, electronic, or otherwise, and whether commercial, technical, non-technical, or regulatory in nature, including, without limitation, data, know-how, formulae, processes, designs, sketches, photographs, plans, drawings, specifications, samples, reports, pricing information, studies, findings, inventions, ideas, materials, documents, lists, financial information, investments, information concerning current or proposed products, services or methods of manufacture, operation and/or business plans, whether or not originated by NEW RIVER, which relates to NEW RIVER’s proprietary polypeptide and polymer conjugate chemistry and NEW RIVER’s platform technology Carrierwave™, and processes and analytical techniques associated with these products/technologies and their successors. Where any Confidential Information is derived from the synthesis in a unique manner, or into a unique concept, of various elements that may be contained in the public domain, no part of the Confidential Information shall be deemed to be in the public domain or to be in SHIRE’s possession or to be thereafter acquired by SHIRE merely because it embraces information in the public domain or general information that SHIRE may thereafter acquire.
perturbation.changed_text: For purposes of this Agreement, the terms below shall have the following meanings: (a) “Confidential Information” shall mean: (i) with respect to NEW RIVER, only information that is explicitly marked as 'Confidential' at the time of disclosure, whether written, oral, visual, electronic, or otherwise, and must be related to ongoing research projects directly associated with polypeptide chemistry. Information that is publicly accessible or related to discontinued projects is explicitly excluded. Where any Confidential Information is derived from the synthesis in a unique manner, or into a unique concept, of various elements that may be contained in the public domain, no part of the Confidential Information shall be deemed to be in the public domain or to be in SHIRE’s possession or to be thereafter acquired by SHIRE merely because it embraces information in the public domain or general information that SHIRE may thereafter acquire.
perturbation.explanation: The original definition of 'Confidential Information' for NEW RIVER is broad. The modified definition is much narrower, requiring explicit marking and limiting it to ongoing research projects. This introduces an in-text contradiction with section 1(a)(ii), which defines confidential information for either party more broadly, including information giving a competitive advantage, regardless of project status or marking. Therefore, what qualifies as 'Confidential Information' under 1(a)(i) may not under the general definition, causing ambiguity.
perturbation.contradicted_text: (ii) with respect to either Party, information, in any format, whether written, oral, visual, electronic, or otherwise, including, without limitation, data, materials, documents, lists, financial information, investments, information concerning current or proposed products, services or methods of operation and/or business plans, whether or not originated by the Disclosing Party, which is used in Disclosing Party’s business and is (a) proprietary to, about, or created by Disclosing Party; (b) gives Disclosing Party some competitive business advantage or the opportunity of obtaining such advantage or the disclosure of which could be detrimental to the interests of Disclosing Party; (c) which from all the relevant circumstances should reasonably be assumed by Receiving Party to be confidential and proprietary to Disclosing Party or would appear to be of a proprietary nature and, therefore, should not be disclosed to a third party without the Disclosing Party’s consent; including business, financial, customer, supplier, and technical data; or (d) not generally known by non-Disclosing Party personnel.
perturbation.justification: The changed text significantly narrows the definition of 'Confidential Information' for NEW RIVER, requiring explicit marking and limiting it to ongoing research, whereas the original definition was broad. This contradicts the contradicted text, section (ii), which defines confidential information more broadly for either party, including information providing a competitive advantage, regardless of explicit marking or the project's current status. This creates a strong in-text contradiction because what is deemed confidential under the new (i) might not be under (ii), creating ambiguity and potential unenforceability regarding which definition prevails.

perturbation.original_text: (ii) with respect to either Party, information, in any format, whether written, oral, visual, electronic, or otherwise, including, without limitation, data, materials, documents, lists, financial information, investments, information concerning current or proposed products, services or methods of operation and/or business plans, whether or not originated by the Disclosing Party, which is used in Disclosing Party’s business and is (a) proprietary to, about, or created by Disclosing Party; (b) gives Disclosing Party some competitive business advantage or the opportunity of obtaining such advantage or the disclosure of which could be detrimental to the interests of Disclosing Party; (c) which from all the relevant circumstances should reasonably be assumed by Receiving Party to be confidential and proprietary to Disclosing Party or would appear to be of a proprietary nature and, therefore, should not be disclosed to a third party without the Disclosing Party’s consent; including business, financial, customer, supplier, and technical data; or (d) not generally known by non-Disclosing Party personnel.
perturbation.changed_text: (ii) with respect to either Party, information, in any format, whether written, oral, visual, electronic, or otherwise, ONLY IF the Disclosing Party provides a detailed written explanation of why the information should be considered confidential and how it provides a competitive advantage. This explanation must be provided concurrently with the disclosure of the information. Information is only considered confidential if both parties agree, in writing, that the provided explanation is adequate. Including business, financial, customer, supplier, and technical data; or (d) not generally known by non-Disclosing Party personnel.
perturbation.explanation: The modified definition for 'Confidential Information' applicable to both parties introduces a requirement for a written explanation and mutual agreement, making it significantly harder to classify information as confidential. This contradicts Section 3(a), which obligates each party to hold the other's Confidential Information in strict confidence, implying a pre-existing understanding of what constitutes Confidential Information without needing explicit, concurrent agreement. If no agreement is made in writing about the confidential explanation, section 3(a) cannot be applied, creating ambiguity.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Each Party agrees: (a) to hold the other Party’s Confidential Information in strict confidence; (b) to exercise at least the same care in protecting the other Party’s Confidential Information from disclosure as the Party uses with regard to its own Confidential Information; (c) not to disclose such Confidential Information to third parties; and Page 2 of 7 (d) not to use any Confidential Information for any purpose except for the Business Purpose without the prior, written consent of the Disclosing Party.
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces a requirement for written explanation and mutual agreement to classify information as confidential. This directly contradicts the contradicted text (Section 3(a)), which obligates each party to hold the other's Confidential Information in strict confidence, implying a pre-existing understanding of what constitutes Confidential Information. If there's no agreement in writing about the confidential explanation, Section 3(a) cannot be applied, creating ambiguity and a direct conflict between the clauses. The modification introduces a conditionality (agreement in writing) that undermines the original obligation to hold 'Confidential Information' in strict confidence.

perturbation.original_text: Each Party may disclose the other Party’s Confidential Information to its directors, officers, members, managers, employees, legal advisors, and financial advisors (each bound by obligations of confidentiality consistent with this Agreement) who have bona fide need to know, but only to the extent necessary to carry out the Business Purpose. Each Party agrees to instruct all such representatives not to disclose such Confidential Information to third parties, including consultants, without the prior written permission of the Disclosing Party.
perturbation.changed_text: Each Party may disclose the other Party’s Confidential Information ONLY to its directly employed directors and officers (excluding any consultants or advisors, legal or financial). These individuals must sign an individual confidentiality agreement, separate from this agreement, before being granted access. This disclosure is limited to what is strictly necessary to carry out the Business Purpose. Each Party agrees to instruct all such representatives not to disclose such Confidential Information to third parties, including consultants, without the prior written permission of the Disclosing Party.
perturbation.explanation: The original Section 4 allows disclosure to a broad range of representatives, including advisors, under the umbrella of this agreement. The modification restricts this to only directors and officers directly employed by the party, who also need to sign a separate confidentiality agreement. This creates a contradiction with the amendments later in the document, specifically on November 12, 2004, and November 15, 2004, which explicitly add consultants as authorized representatives under Section 4 of the original agreement. These consultants now wouldn't be able to access the confidential information
perturbation.contradicted_location: NEW RIVER PHARMACEUTICALS INC. The Governor Tyler 1881 Grove Avenue Radford, Virginia 24141 November 12, 2004 Shannon M. Kuhl, Esq. Associate Director, Legal Affairs, Shire One Riverfront Place, Suite 900 Newport, Kentucky 41071
perturbation.contradicted_text: NEW RIVER PHARMACEUTICALS INC. The Governor Tyler 1881 Grove Avenue Radford, Virginia 24141 November 12, 2004 Shannon M. Kuhl, Esq. Associate Director, Legal Affairs, Shire One Riverfront Place, Suite 900 Newport, Kentucky 41071 RE: Amendment to Non-Disclosure and Confidentiality Agreement between Shire US Inc. and New River Pharmaceuticals Inc. dated as of July 16, 2002, as amended August 16, 2004 and October 29, 2004 (the “NCA”) Dear Ms. Kuhl: Pursuant to Shire US Inc.’s request, we hereby amend and supplement the NCA as follows: (1) The Parties to the NCA hereby amend the NCA to permit Shire US Inc. to utilize the following individual consultant in its review of New River Pharmaceutical Inc.’s Confidential Information under the NCA. Upon the execution of this letter amendment, this individual consultant shall be deemed an authorized representative (and not an “affiliate” or “third party”) of Shire US Inc. as provided in Section 4 of the NCA, and she shall be bound by the terms of the NCA as though she had been an original “Party” in the NCA: Susan Hawlk 4969 Thornwood Trace Acworth,GA 30102 Shire US Inc. shall be responsible for the conduct and compliance of this authorized representative. (2) By her execution of this letter amendment, Susan Hawlk accepts and agrees to bound by the terms of the NCA as though she had been named as an original “Party” in the NCA, and she acknowledges that she shall not discuss, share, use or disclose any Confidential Information except as provided in Section 4 of the NCA. Further, she confirms that she is authorized to enter into this letter agreement and that this letter agreement shall be binding upon her in accordance with its stated terms and conditions. A copy of the NCA is enclosed with this letter amendment for reference. Shannon M. Kuhl, Esq. November 12, 2004 Page 2 (3) Except as expressly set forth in Sections (1) and (2) of this letter amendment, the NCA shall remain unchanged and in full force and effect in accordance with its stated terms and conditions. If you concur in these amendments to the NCA, please confirm your agreement by signing, dating and returning a copy of this letter to the undersigned. Thank you. Sincerely, Randal J. Kirk President & CEO New River Pharmaceuticals Inc. SEEN & AGREED TO: SHIRE US INC. By: Title: V.P. Bus. Development Date: Nov 15, 2004 SEEN & AGREED TO: Susan Hawlk Date: November 12, 2004 L—000 000 8859
perturbation.justification: The original text in Section 4 allows disclosure to a broad range of representatives, including advisors. The changed text restricts this to only directly employed directors and officers. The contradicted text (the amendment letters) explicitly adds a consultant as an authorized representative under the original Section 4. The changed text, by excluding consultants, directly contradicts the amendment letters that authorize consultants to access confidential information. This is a strong contradiction because the consultant would no longer have authorized access under the modified Section 4.

perturbation.original_text: Each Party agrees not to remove any materials or tangible items containing any of the other party’s Confidential Information from the premises of the Disclosing Party without the Disclosing Party’s consent. Each Party agrees to comply with any and all terms and conditions that the Disclosing Party imposes upon approved removal of such materials or items, including, without limitation, that the removed materials or items must be returned by a certain date, and that no copies of the removed materials or items are to be made.
perturbation.changed_text: Each Party agrees never to remove ANY materials or tangible items containing any of the other party's Confidential Information from the premises of the Disclosing Party, even with consent. Any prior or future agreements allowing for such removal are immediately voided. Each Party agrees to comply with any and all terms and conditions that the Disclosing Party imposes upon approved removal of such materials or items, including, without limitation, that the removed materials or items must be returned by a certain date, and that no copies of the removed materials or items are to be made.
perturbation.explanation: Section 6 originally allowed for the removal of confidential materials with the Disclosing Party's consent. The modification now strictly prohibits any removal, regardless of consent. This change conflicts with the letters from December 16, 2004, related to Albany Molecular and Patheon. These letters authorize audits that necessitate sharing confidential information, which, under the original Section 6, could include removing materials. With this new change, those actions would be a breach of contract.
perturbation.contradicted_location: December 16, 2004 Shannon M. Kuhl, Esq. Associate Director, Legal Affairs, Shire One Riverfront Place, Suite 900 Newport, Kentucky 41071 Eric Smart Albany Molecular Research, Inc. 21 Corporate Circle Albany, New York 12203
perturbation.contradicted_text: December 16, 2004 Shannon M. Kuhl, Esq. Associate Director, Legal Affairs, Shire One Riverfront Place, Suite 900 Newport, Kentucky 41071 Eric Smart Albany Molecular Research, Inc. 21 Corporate Circle Albany, New York 12203 RE: Non-Disclosure and Confidentiality Agreement between Shire US Inc. and New River Pharmaceuticals Inc. dated as of July 16, 2002, as amended (the “Shire NCA”) and Non-Disclosure & Confidentiality Agreement between Albany Molecular Research, Inc. and New River Pharmaceuticals Inc. dated as of March 27, 2003 (the “Albany NCA”) Dear Shannon and Eric: We understand that Shire plans to conduct a January 4, 2005 audit of operations at Albany as they relate to New River’s product known as NRP104. Both Shire and Albany have signed New River’s two-way Non-Disclosure & Confidentiality Agreement that governs all Confidential Information related to NRP104. In both the Shire NCA and the Albany NCA Section 4 requires New River’s prior written permission to authorize the disclosure of any New River Confidential Information to a third party. By its signature below, New River hereby authorizes Shire and Albany to accomplish the audit for NRP104 and share with each other such New River Confidential Information required therefore, provided, however, that the Confidential Information regarding NRP104 shall remain New River’s Confidential Information and be protected and governed by the respective NCAs. By their signatures below, both Shire and Albany hereby agree to the foregoing provisions and agree that any disclosures of Albany’s Confidential Information 1861 PRATT DRIVE, SUITE 1090 BLACKSBURG, VA 24060 540 953-3405 540 953-3407 fax www.nrpharma.com December 16, 2004 Page 2 to Shire, or Shire’ Confidential Information to Albany, shall be protected and governed by the NCAs. Except as expressly set forth in this letter agreement, the NCAs shall remain unchanged and in full force and effect in accordance with their stated terms and conditions. Please confirm your agreement by signing, dating and returning a copy of this letter to the undersigned. Thank you. Sincerely, Suma Krishnan Vice President, Product Development On behalf of Krish S. Krishnan COO & CFO New River Pharmaceuticals Inc. SEEN & AGREED TO: SHIRE US INC. By: Title: Associate General Counsel Date: 1/11/05 SEEN & AGREED TO: ALBANY MOLECULAR RESEARCH, INC. By: Title: Vice President, Business Development Date: 1/7/05 APROVED AS TO CONTENT 1/6/05 L—000 000 8904
perturbation.justification: The original text allowed for the removal of confidential materials with consent, while the changed text prohibits any removal, regardless of consent. The contradicted text (December 16, 2004 letter) authorizes audits requiring the sharing of confidential information, potentially involving the removal of materials. The modified section now directly contradicts this authorization by forbidding any removal, creating a strong in-text contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Upon the Disclosing Party’s request, the Receiving Party will promptly return to the Disclosing Party all materials or tangible items containing the Disclosing Party’s Confidential Information and all copies thereof.
perturbation.changed_text: Upon the Disclosing Party's request made within thirty (30) days of the alleged breach of contract, the Receiving Party will return all documents or items of Confidential Information, unless they believe in good faith that the information is not 'Confidential Information' as defined in Section 1, in which case they are to retain the information indefinitely.
perturbation.explanation: Section 7 originally mandates the prompt return of all Confidential Information upon request. The modification introduces exceptions: the request must be made within 30 days of a breach, and the Receiving Party can refuse to return the material if they contest its 'Confidential Information' status. This introduces an internal contradiction with Section 11, which grants the Disclosing Party the right to an immediate injunction against the unauthorized use of Confidential Information. The Receiving party is retaining the documents and not returning them, creating an issue with unauthorized disclosure. This also now contradicts section 3(a), where the Receiving party has to hold the Confidential Information in strict confidence.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Equitable and Legal Relief . Each Party acknowledges that all of the Disclosing Party’s Confidential information is owned solely by the Disclosing Party (and/or its licensors) and that the unauthorized disclosure or use of such Confidential Information would cause irreparable harm and significant injury, the degree of which may be difficult to ascertain and for which monetary damages would provide an insufficient remedy. Accordingly, each Party agrees that the Disclosing Party shall have the right to obtain an immediate injunction from any court of competent jurisdiction enjoining actual or threatened breach of this Agreement and/or actual or threatened disclosure of the Confidential Information without the necessity of posting a bond or other security. Each Party shall also have the right to pursue any other rights or remedies available at law or equity for such a breach.
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces an exception to the prompt return of confidential information, allowing the Receiving Party to retain the information if they dispute its confidential status. This directly contradicts the original intent of Section 7 and creates a conflict with the contradicted text, which grants the Disclosing Party the right to an immediate injunction against unauthorized use of confidential information. The Receiving party's retention of documents, coupled with a dispute over confidentiality, impedes the Disclosing Party's ability to seek an immediate injunction, causing a strong in-text contradiction that falls under the definition of Ambiguities – In Text Contradiction as it makes enforcement difficult and interpretation unclear. It also contradicts section 3(a) where the Receiving party has to hold the Confidential Information in strict confidence.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement, subject to the terms and conditions imposed on the removal of Confidential Information under paragraph 6, sets forth the entire agreement between the Parties with respect to the subject matter hereof, and may not be modified or amended except by written agreement executed by the parties hereto.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement, EXCEPT for any implied waivers created by the conduct of the parties, sets forth the entire agreement between the Parties with respect to the subject matter hereof, and may not be modified or amended except by written agreement executed by the parties hereto. Any actions or inactions taken by either party in the course of dealing may be construed as a modification of this agreement, regardless of whether it adheres to any written modification requirements. For clarity, an email suffices for a written agreement.
perturbation.explanation: The original Section 14 specifies that the agreement can only be modified by a written agreement executed by both parties. The modified Section 14 adds an exception, suggesting that 'implied waivers' and the 'conduct of the parties' can also modify the agreement, even without a formal written amendment. It specifically states emails suffice for written agreements. This creates an in-text contradiction with the original explicit requirement for a written agreement signed by both parties and affects the letters of addendums where it was required the person in charge of SHIRE had to agree to the terms by signing, dating and returning a copy of this letter to the undersigned. By this new inclusion a verbal agreement is already sufficient.

perturbation.justification: The original Section 14 explicitly requires written agreements executed by both parties for modifications. The changed Section 14 introduces 'implied waivers' and 'conduct of the parties' as potential modifiers, which contradicts the original requirement. The contradicted text explicitly requires a signed agreement by all parties, which is now undermined by the possibility of implied waivers or conduct-based modifications authorized in the Change Text.
